title: FIREWALL RULE ENGINE v3.1
description:
== FIREWALL RULE ENGINE v3.1 ==
STATUS: WIPED BY [NULL]

[NULL] erased the firewall's rule engine. Packets flow unchecked. Restore it.

RULE FORMAT:  "ACTION|port_min-port_max|protocol"
  - ACTION is "ALLOW" or "DENY"
  - port_min and port_max define an inclusive range
  - protocol is a string like "TCP" or "UDP"

PACKET FORMAT:  "port|protocol"

LOGIC: If the packet's port is within [port_min, port_max] AND the protocol matches (case-insensitive), return the ACTION. Otherwise return "NO_MATCH".

Input: line 1 is the rule string, line 2 is Q (number of packets), next Q lines are packet strings
Output: Q lines, each with the result for that packet

title: FIREWALL RULE ENGINE v3.2
description:
== FIREWALL RULE ENGINE v3.2 ==
STATUS: WIPED BY [NULL]

[NULL] erased the IP-based rule engine. Traffic flows unfiltered. Restore it.

RULE FORMAT:  "ACTION|ip_prefix|port"
  - ACTION is "ALLOW" or "DENY"
  - ip_prefix is a partial IP like "192.168" — a packet IP matches if it STARTS WITH this string
  - port is an exact port number

PACKET FORMAT:  "ip_address|port"

LOGIC: If the packet's IP starts with ip_prefix AND the port matches exactly, return the ACTION. Otherwise return "NO_MATCH".

Note: prefix matching is a plain string prefix check — "192.168.10" also matches "192.168.100.x".

Input: line 1 is the rule string, line 2 is Q (number of packets), next Q lines are packet strings
Output: Q lines, each with the result for that packet

title: FIREWALL RULE ENGINE v3.3
description:
== FIREWALL RULE ENGINE v3.3 ==
STATUS: WIPED BY [NULL]

[NULL] erased the outbound traffic filter. Every packet escapes unchecked. Restore it.

RULE FORMAT:  "ACTION|protocol|min_port"
  - ACTION is "ALLOW" or "DENY"
  - protocol is a string like "TCP", "UDP", "ICMP"
  - min_port is a threshold — the rule applies to packets with port >= min_port

PACKET FORMAT:  "protocol|port"

LOGIC: If the protocol matches (case-insensitive) AND the packet's port >= min_port, return the ACTION. Otherwise return "NO_MATCH".

Input: line 1 is the rule string, line 2 is Q (number of packets), next Q lines are packet strings
Output: Q lines, each with the result for that packet

title: FIREWALL RULE ENGINE v3.4
description:
== FIREWALL RULE ENGINE v3.4 ==
STATUS: WIPED BY [NULL]

[NULL] erased the zone-based packet filter. Internal zones bleed into external. Restore it.

RULE FORMAT:  "ACTION|zone|port_min-port_max"
  - ACTION is "ALLOW" or "DENY"
  - zone is a string like "INTERNAL", "EXTERNAL", "DMZ"
  - port_min and port_max define an inclusive port range

PACKET FORMAT:  "zone|port"

LOGIC: If the zone matches (case-insensitive) AND the port is within [port_min, port_max], return the ACTION. Otherwise return "NO_MATCH".

Input: line 1 is the rule string, line 2 is Q (number of packets), next Q lines are packet strings
Output: Q lines, each with the result for that packet

title: DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATOR v2.0
description:
== DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATOR v2.0 ==
STATUS: CHAIN SEVERED BY [NULL]

[NULL] corrupted the dynamic memory allocator's linked structure. Rebuild the node definition and the reversal engine.

Requirements:
1. Define a static inner class "Node" inside MemoryAllocator with: an int field "data", a Node field "next", and a constructor that takes an int and sets next to null.
2. Implement: public static String buildAndReverse(int[] values)
   - Build a singly linked list from the array (first element = head).
   - Reverse the entire list in-place using pointer manipulation (no extra collections).
   - Return the reversed list as "data1 -> data2 -> data3".
   - Return "EMPTY" if the input array has no elements.

Input: a single line of space-separated integers, or the word "EMPTY" for an empty list
Output: the reversed linked list as a string, or "EMPTY"

title: DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATOR v2.1
description:
== DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATOR v2.1 ==
STATUS: CORRUPTED BY [NULL]

[NULL] destroyed the memory chain analyzer. Length is unknown and the midpoint is lost. Rebuild the chain, count it, and locate the center.

Requirements:
1. Define a static inner class "Node" inside MemoryAllocator with: an int field "data", a Node field "next", and a constructor that takes an int and sets next to null.
2. Implement: public static String buildAndFindMiddle(int[] values)
   - Build a singly linked list from the array.
   - Find the middle node using the slow/fast pointer technique.
     For even-length lists, return the SECOND of the two middle nodes.
   - Return "LENGTH:n|MIDDLE:data" where n is the total number of nodes.
   - Return "EMPTY" if the input array has no elements.

Input: a single line of space-separated integers, or the word "EMPTY" for an empty list
Output: "LENGTH:n|MIDDLE:data" or "EMPTY"

title: DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATOR v2.2
description:
== DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATOR v2.2 ==
STATUS: POLLUTED BY [NULL]

[NULL] injected duplicate entries into the memory chain. Repeated allocations corrupt the heap. Rebuild the chain and strip all duplicates, keeping only the first occurrence of each value.

Requirements:
1. Define a static inner class "Node" inside MemoryAllocator with: an int field "data", a Node field "next", and a constructor that takes an int and sets next to null.
2. Implement: public static String buildAndDeduplicate(int[] values)
   - Build a singly linked list from the array.
   - Remove all duplicate values, keeping only the FIRST occurrence of each.
   - Return the deduplicated list as "data1 -> data2 -> data3".
   - Return "EMPTY" if the input array has no elements.

Input: a single line of space-separated integers, or the word "EMPTY" for an empty list
Output: deduplicated linked list as a string, or "EMPTY"

title: DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATOR v2.3
description:
== DYNAMIC MEMORY ALLOCATOR v2.3 ==
STATUS: INFECTED BY [NULL]

[NULL] planted rogue entries in the memory chain. A known malicious value is embedded at random positions. Rebuild the chain and surgically remove every occurrence of the target.

Requirements:
1. Define a static inner class "Node" inside MemoryAllocator with: an int field "data", a Node field "next", and a constructor that takes an int and sets next to null.
2. Implement: public static String buildAndPurge(int[] values, int target)
   - Build a singly linked list from the array.
   - Remove ALL nodes whose data equals target.
   - Return the cleaned list as "data1 -> data2 -> data3".
   - Return "EMPTY" if the list is empty after purging (or if the input was empty).

Input: line 1 is a space-separated list of integers, or "EMPTY" for an empty list; line 2 is the target integer to remove
Output: purged linked list as a string, or "EMPTY"

title: SYSTEM BOOT SEQUENCER v1.0
description:
== SYSTEM BOOT SEQUENCER v1.0 ==
STATUS: SCRAMBLED BY [NULL]

[NULL] scrambled the boot sequence. Services start before their dependencies, causing cascade failures. Reconstruct the topological order — every dependency must load first.

Implement bootOrder(int numServices, int[][] deps) using Kahn's algorithm (BFS-based topological sort):
- Track how many unresolved dependencies each service has (in-degree).
- Use a min-priority queue so that when multiple services are ready, the LOWEST-numbered boots first.
- Remove each booted service from others' dependency counts.
- If not all services were booted, there is a cycle — return "CYCLE_DETECTED".
- Otherwise return a space-separated boot order.

Input: line 1 = N (number of services, labeled 0 to N-1), line 2 = M (number of dependency pairs), next M lines = "a b" meaning service a depends on service b (b boots before a)
Output: space-separated boot order, or "CYCLE_DETECTED"

title: SYSTEM BOOT SEQUENCER v1.1
description:
== SYSTEM BOOT SEQUENCER v1.1 ==
STATUS: INVERTED BY [NULL]

[NULL] reversed the boot priority. The system now demands the highest-numbered service boots first when multiple are ready. Reconstruct the topological order with inverted priority.

Implement bootOrder(int numServices, int[][] deps) — same logic as v1.0 but use a MAX-priority queue so that when multiple services are ready at the same time, the HIGHEST-numbered boots first.

Example: 4 services, deps: 1→0, 2→0, 3→2
- Only 0 is ready first. Boot 0.
- Both 1 and 2 become ready. Pick 2 (highest). Boot 2.
- Both 1 and 3 become ready. Pick 3 (highest). Boot 3.
- Only 1 remains. Boot 1.
- Result: 0 2 3 1

Input: line 1 = N (number of services), line 2 = M (dependency pairs), next M lines = "a b" (a depends on b)
Output: space-separated boot order (highest-first tiebreak), or "CYCLE_DETECTED"

title: SYSTEM BOOT SEQUENCER v1.2
description:
== SYSTEM BOOT SEQUENCER v1.2 ==
STATUS: DEPTH UNKNOWN BY [NULL]

[NULL] scrambled the boot sequence and erased the layer count. Without knowing how deep the dependency chain goes, staged rollout is impossible. Reconstruct the boot order AND report the number of boot layers.

Implement bootOrder(int numServices, int[][] deps) — same as v1.0 (lowest-first tiebreak) but process all currently-ready services in one round (one layer) before starting the next:
- Round 1: boot all services with no dependencies, smallest first.
- Round 2: boot all newly-unblocked services, smallest first. Etc.
- Return two lines: boot order (all services space-separated) then "LAYERS:X".
- If a cycle exists, return only "CYCLE_DETECTED".

Example: 4 services, deps: 1→0, 2→0, 3→2
- Round 1: [0]. Round 2: [1, 2]. Round 3: [3]. → "0 1 2 3\nLAYERS:3"

Input: line 1 = N, line 2 = M, next M lines = "a b" (a depends on b)
Output: space-separated boot order on line 1, "LAYERS:X" on line 2, or just "CYCLE_DETECTED"

title: SYSTEM SHUTDOWN SEQUENCER v1.0
description:
== SYSTEM SHUTDOWN SEQUENCER v1.0 ==
STATUS: CORRUPTED BY [NULL]

[NULL] scrambled the shutdown sequence. Services that others depend on are killed first, crashing everything downstream. Reconstruct the REVERSE topological order: dependents shut down before their dependencies.

Implement shutdownOrder(int numServices, int[][] deps) — this is topological sort on the reverse graph:
- A service can only shut down when nothing else depends on it (out-degree = 0 in the original graph).
- Use a min-priority queue so the LOWEST-numbered eligible service shuts down first.
- Return the space-separated shutdown order, or "CYCLE_DETECTED" if a cycle exists.

Example: 4 services, deps: 1→0, 2→0, 3→2
- Services with no dependents initially: {1, 3}. Pick 1 (lowest). Shut down 1.
- After 1: {3}. Shut down 3. After 3: {2}. Shut down 2. After 2: {0}. Shut down 0.
- Result: 1 3 2 0

Input: line 1 = N (number of services), line 2 = M (dependency pairs), next M lines = "a b" (a depends on b)
Output: space-separated shutdown order, or "CYCLE_DETECTED"
